---
title: "Time Series Project"
author: "Group 1"
date: "2026-01-31"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)

summary(mtcars)

library(dplyr)

```

#Import datasets

```{r}

library(readr)
price_data <- read_csv("3210007701_databaseLoadingData.csv")
head(price_data)
```

```{r}
data2 <- read_csv("3210035101_databaseLoadingData_2.csv")
head(data2)
```

#1.2 Graphical exploration (delivery data)

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

delivery_data = data.frame(Date = data2$REF_DATE,
                           Value = as.numeric(data2$VALUE))

head(delivery_data)
p2 = ggplot(delivery_data, aes(x=Date, y = Value, group=1))+
             geom_line(color="blue", size=1)+
  labs(
    title = "Producer deliveries of Wheat, Excluding Durum",
    subtitle = "2002-2025",
    x = "Date (Monthly)",
    y = "Grain (Tonnes)",
    caption = "Source: Statistics Canada. Table 32-10-0351-01  Producer deliveries of major grains"
  ) 
print(p2)
```

#1.2 Graphical exploration (price data)

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)

price_data <- price_data %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(paste0(REF_DATE, "-01")))

price_data <- price_data %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_price_canada = mean(VALUE, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(price_data, aes(x = date, y = avg_price_canada)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Average Prices of Wheat in Canada",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Average Price"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
print(price_data)

```

#2.1 Removing the Trend (Delivery data)

```{r}
delivery_data_D1 = diff(delivery_data$Value)
delivery_data_D2 = diff(delivery_data_D1)
delivery_data_D3 = diff(log(delivery_data$Value))

par(mfrow=c(2,2))

#Plot Original Graph
plot(delivery_data$Value, type = 'l', xlab = "Date", ylab = "Wheat (Tonnes)", main = "Original Data")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)

# First-Order Differencing
plot( delivery_data_D1, type = 'l', xlab = 'Date', ylab = "Wheat (Tonnes)", main = "First-order Differencing")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)

# Second-Order differencing
plot( delivery_data_D2, type = 'l', xlab = 'Date', ylab = "Wheat (Tonnes)", main = "Second-Order Differencing")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)

# Third-Order differencing
plot( delivery_data_D3, type = 'l', xlab = 'Date', ylab = "Wheat (Tonnes)", main = "Log Differencing")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)

#Log Differencing is the best option.
```

# 2.2 Removing Seasonal Effects (delivery data)

```{r}
library(forecast)

acf(delivery_data_D3, 
    main="ACF of diff series",
    lag.max = 48,
    xlab = "Months",
    xlim=c(1,48)
  )

#There are periodic spikes in the ACF graph at multiples of 12 months, so we will use lag=12 to remove the seasonality.

delivery_data_deseasonalized=diff(delivery_data_D3, lag=12)



plot( delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = 'l', xlab = 'Date', ylab = "Wheat (Tonnes)", main = "Deseasonalized")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)



```

#2.3 Analysis of differenced Series (delivery data)

```{r}
mean=mean(delivery_data_deseasonalized)
varience = var(delivery_data_deseasonalized)

std_dev = sqrt(varience)

lower = qnorm(mean = mean, sd = std_dev, p=0.05)
upper = qnorm(mean = mean, sd = std_dev, p=0.95)

plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = 'l', main = 'Delivery data, deseasonalized', xlab = 'month', ylab = "Wheat shipped, tonnes")
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)
abline(h = lower, col = 'red', lty = 2)
abline(h = upper, col = 'red', lty = 2)

#Check if 95% is within.
number=0
for(n in 1:length(delivery_data_deseasonalized)){
  if(delivery_data_deseasonalized[n]>upper){
    number=number+1
  }
  if(delivery_data_deseasonalized[n]<lower){
    number=number+1
  }
}
 number
 #26 out of 275 (about 9%) of the values lie outside this range. If the process is stationary, about 5% (13.75) should lie outside this interval on average.
 
 acf(delivery_data_deseasonalized, 
    main="ACF of deseasonalized series",
    lag.max = 48,
    xlab = "Months",
    xlim=c(1,48)
  )
 #Most of the values in the Autocorrelation function are close to 0, which implies that there is no autocorrelation.
 
```

#3.1

```{r}

library(zoo)

# Define q ranges
q_grid_var <- 1:10
q_grid_rmse <- 1:10

# Variance after smoothing
vars <- sapply(q_grid_var, function(q) {
  var(rollmean(delivery_data_deseasonalized, k = q, fill = NA), na.rm = TRUE)
})

plot(q_grid_var, vars, type = "b",
     xlab = "q", ylab = "Variance after smoothing",
     main = "Variance Reduction vs q")

# RMSE after smoothing
rmse_results <- sapply(q_grid_rmse, function(q) {
  sm <- rollmean(delivery_data_deseasonalized, k = q, fill = NA)
  sqrt(mean((delivery_data_deseasonalized - sm)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
})

plot(q_grid_rmse, rmse_results, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "blue",
     xlab = "Smoothing Parameter (q)", 
     ylab = "RMSE", 
     main = "RMSE vs. Parameter q")

# Apply chosen q, 2 plots to compare
q <- 5
q2 = 4

smoothed <- rollmean(delivery_data_deseasonalized, k = q, fill = NA)
smoothed2 <- rollmean(delivery_data_deseasonalized, k = q2, fill = NA)

zoom_start = 0
zoom_end = 100

plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = "l",
     col = "gray50", lwd = 1,
     main = paste("Linear Moving Average Filter (q =", q, ")"),
     xlab = "Time", ylab = "Value",
     xlim = c(zoom_start, zoom_end))

lines(smoothed, col = "red", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Moving Average"),
       col = c("gray50", "red"),
       lwd = c(1, 2),
       bty = "n")

grid()

#Second plot to compare with


plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = "l",
     col = "gray50", lwd = 1,
     main = paste("Linear Moving Average Filter (q2 =", q2, ")"),
     xlab = "Time", ylab = "Value",
     xlim = c(zoom_start, zoom_end))

lines(smoothed2, col = "red", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Moving Average"),
       col = c("gray50", "red"),
       lwd = c(1, 2),
       bty = "n")

grid()

```

```{r}
 acf(delivery_ts, 
    main="ACF of deseasonalized series",
    lag.max = 48,
    xlab = "Months",
    xlim=c(1,48)
  )
```

```{r}

library(forecast)




# Suppose your time series is called 'ts_data'

# Grid of alpha values to test
alpha_values <- seq(0.1, 0.5, by = 0.05)

# Function to compute MSE for a given alpha
mse_for_alpha <- function(alpha) {
  model <- ses(delivery_data_deseasonalized, alpha = alpha, initial = "simple", h = 1)
  mean((delivery_data_deseasonalized - fitted(model))^2)
}

# Compute MSE for each alpha
errors <- sapply(alpha_values, mse_for_alpha)

# Find alpha with the minimum MSE
best_alpha <- alpha_values[which.min(errors)]
best_alpha


alpha_value <- 0.5
exp_series <- ses(delivery_data_deseasonalized, alpha = alpha_value, initial = "simple")$fitted

plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = "l",
     col = "gray50", lwd = 1,
     main = "Exponential Smoothing Filter",
     xlab = "Time", ylab = "Value")

lines(exp_series, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Exponential Smoothed"),
       col = c("gray50", "blue"),
       lwd = c(1, 2),
       bty = "n")

grid()

alpha_value <- 0.5
exp_series <- ses(delivery_data_deseasonalized, alpha = alpha_value, initial = "simple")$fitted

# Define your zoom window here
zoom_start <- 100
zoom_end <- 150

plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = "l",
     col = "gray50", lwd = 1,
     main = "Exponential Smoothing Filter (Zoomed View)",
     xlab = "Time", ylab = "Value",
     xlim = c(zoom_start, zoom_end)) # This line performs the "slice"

lines(exp_series, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Exponential Smoothed"),
       col = c("gray50", "blue"),
       lwd = c(1, 2),
       bty = "n")

grid()



```

```{r}
zoom_start = 100
zoom_end = 150

plot(delivery_data_deseasonalized, type = "p",
     col = "gray60", pch = 16, cex = 0.6,
     main = "Comparison of Filtering Techniques",
     xlab = "Time", ylab = "Value",
     xlim = c(zoom_start, zoom_end))

lines(smoothed, col = "red", lwd = 2)
lines(exp_series, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Original", "Moving Average", "Exponential Smoothing"),
       col = c("gray60", "red", "blue"),
       pch = c(16, NA, NA),
       lwd = c(NA, 2, 2),
       pt.cex = c(0.8, NA, NA),
       bty = "n")

grid()






```

```{r}
#with lab

delivery_ts <- ts(data2$VALUE,
                  start = c(2002,1), # Start year and month: January 2002
                  frequency = 12) # 12 observations per year (monthly data)


# Define moving average filter function
moving_average <- function(X, q) {
  N <- length(X)              # Length of input series
  weight <- 1 / (2 * q + 1)   # Equal weights for symmetric MA
  indices <- (q+1):(N-q)      # Indices where filter can be applied
  X_filt <- rep(NA, N)        # Initialize output with NAs
  
  # Apply moving average filter
  for (i in indices) {
    X_filt[i] <- sum(X[(i-q):(i+q)] * weight)
  }
  return(X_filt)               # Return filtered series
}

# Apply moving average to your existing time series
X_filt1 <- ts(moving_average(delivery_ts, 6),
              start = start(delivery_ts),
              frequency = frequency(delivery_ts))

X_filt2 <- ts(moving_average(delivery_ts, 12),
              start = start(delivery_ts),
              frequency = frequency(delivery_ts))


zoom_start <- 1
zoom_end <- 100

delivery_zoom <- window(delivery_ts, end = time(delivery_ts)[zoom_end])
X_filt1_zoom <- window(X_filt1, end = time(X_filt1)[zoom_end])
X_filt2_zoom <- window(X_filt2, end = time(X_filt2)[zoom_end])


plot.ts(delivery_zoom,
        main = 'Delivery Data Deseasonalized (First 100 Obs)',
        xlab = 'Time',
        ylab = 'Values')

lines(X_filt1_zoom, col = 'red', lwd = 2)
lines(X_filt2_zoom, col = 'blue', lwd = 2)

abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("Original", "q=2", "q=10"),
       col = c('black', 'red', 'blue'),
       lty = 1,
       lwd = 2)




# Plot original series and filtered versions
plot.ts(delivery_ts,
        main = 'Delivery Data Deseasonalized',
        xlab = 'Time',
        ylab = 'Values')
lines(X_filt1, type = 'l', col = 'red')   # Add MA(q=2) in red
lines(X_filt2, type = 'l', col = 'blue')  # Add MA(q=12) in blue
abline(h = 0, col = 'black', lty = 2)     # Reference line at 0
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Original", "q=2", "q=12"),
       col = c('black', 'red', 'blue'),
       lty = 1,
       lwd = 2)



```
